_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Fillmore County, MN. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses — earliest Norwegian settlement county in the cluster, Driftless Area karst landscape.

previous_courthouses.fate: County seat moved to Preston in 1858 after political contest. Early records transferred.
Replaced by brick building. Records survived.
Demolished and replaced by 1955 building. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.description: Fillmore County has no known major courthouse fires or record losses. The county's location in southeastern Minnesota, far from the US-Dakota War combat zone, and the orderly transfer of records through three courthouse transitions have preserved a continuous record from the county's 1853 organization. The county seat contest between Chatfield and Preston did not result in record damage.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} left a paper trail in Fillmore County — a land purchase, a probate filing, a marriage record — it almost certainly survives. The gaps here are in the lives, not in the archive.
record_losses.source: History of Fillmore County, Minnesota. Minneapolis: Minnesota Historical Company, 1882.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous from county organization. Fillmore was one of the earliest organized counties in Minnesota Territory.
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Some early entries are abbreviated.
deeds.repository: Fillmore County Recorder, Preston; Minnesota Historical Society microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com MN land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — probate records from earliest settlement period
wills_estates.gaps: Early estate administrations may be incomplete for families who died intestate without significant property.
wills_estates.repository: Fillmore County Probate Court, Preston; Minnesota Historical Society

marriages.completeness: Good — civil marriage records from county organization
marriages.gaps: Norwegian immigrants frequently married in church ceremonies; civil recording was sometimes delayed or omitted entirely before 1870.
marriages.repository: Fillmore County Recorder; Minnesota Historical Society microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — county court and district court minutes from organization
court_minutes.gaps: Justice of the peace dockets are incomplete, especially for rural townships.
court_minutes.repository: Fillmore County Courthouse; Minnesota Historical Society
court_minutes.online_access: Minnesota Historical Society microfilm; limited online

vital_records.completeness: Minnesota required registration from 1870, but rural compliance lagged. Fillmore County's rural character and Norwegian population meant church records were often the only documentation of births and deaths until the 1890s.
vital_records.repository: Minnesota Department of Health; Fillmore County Recorder; Minnesota Historical Society

naturalization_records.completeness: Good — naturalization records survive from statehood. Important for Norwegian and German immigrant families.
naturalization_records.gaps: Some immigrants filed declarations of intent in other counties before settling in Fillmore.
naturalization_records.repository: Fillmore County District Court; National Archives — Chicago; Minnesota Historical Society

substitute_sources.description: Norwegian Lutheran congregations in Fillmore County — including Highland Prairie, Arendahl, Spring Grove, and numerous Synod and Hauge Synod churches — maintained parish registers with baptisms, confirmations, marriages, burials, and communion records. Highland Prairie Lutheran is among the oldest Norwegian congregations in Minnesota (organized 1851).
substitute_sources.repository: Norwegian-American Historical Association, Northfield; Luther Seminary Archives, St. Paul; individual congregations; FamilySearch microfilm
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for pre-1870 vital events. The ministerialbok often records the immigrant's home parish in Norway. Confirmation records provide age, parents, and sometimes birthplace. Burial records may be the only death documentation before civil registration.
substitute_sources.source: Qualey, Carlton C. Norwegian Settlement in the United States. Northfield, MN: Norwegian-American Historical Association, 1938.

substitute_sources.description: Federal population schedules 1850–1940 and Minnesota state/territorial censuses (1849, 1857, 1865, 1875, 1885, 1895, 1905). The 1857 territorial census captures the earliest Norwegian settlers.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives; FamilySearch; Ancestry.com; Minnesota Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The alternating federal and state censuses provide data points every five years. Agricultural schedules (1850–1880) reveal farm operations in detail.
substitute_sources.source: Minnesota Historical Society. Minnesota Census Records Guide. St. Paul: MHS, 2004.

substitute_sources.source_type: Fillmore County plat books and atlases
substitute_sources.description: Published county atlases (1874, 1896, 1916) and plat maps show landowners by name on individual sections. These are essential for locating families on the landscape.
substitute_sources.repository: Minnesota Historical Society; Fillmore County Historical Society, Fountain; FamilySearch
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Plat maps place {ancestor_name} on a specific piece of ground, linking the name to the land. Comparing multiple atlas editions reveals when families acquired or lost their farms.
substitute_sources.source: Various. Standard Atlas of Fillmore County, Minnesota. Chicago: Geo. A. Ogle & Co., 1896.

narrative_hooks: Fillmore County's records run unbroken from 1853 — among the earliest in Minnesota — and if {ancestor_name} settled here in the first wave of Norwegian immigration, the deed, the marriage record, or the probate file likely still sits in the Preston courthouse.

The Norwegian church records in Fillmore County reach back to 1851, before Minnesota was even a state — if {ancestor_name} was baptized or married at Highland Prairie or Arendahl Lutheran, the ministerialbok entry may be the oldest surviving record of {pronoun_possessive} life in America.

Minnesota began requiring birth and death registration in 1870 — earlier than most states — but in rural Fillmore County, the Norwegian pastor's parish register was often the only record of a birth or burial until the 1890s.

If {ancestor_name} came through Fillmore County on the way west to a later settlement, the naturalization papers may have been filed here first — declarations of intent in Fillmore County, final papers in whatever county they ended up in.
source_references: History of Fillmore County, Minnesota. Minneapolis: Minnesota Historical Company, 1882.
Qualey, Carlton C. Norwegian Settlement in the United States. Northfield, MN: Norwegian-American Historical Association, 1938.
Minnesota Historical Society. Minnesota Census Records Guide. St. Paul: MHS, 2004.
Various. Standard Atlas of Fillmore County, Minnesota. Chicago: Geo. A. Ogle & Co., 1896.
